speaker: interviewer
text: With the refugees coming, did you expect that war would start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew. The refugees come from the other part of Poland. And they come by us. And we took in every-- every house had to take in so many refugees. And they didn't stay too long till the-- till the war broke out between Germany and-- and-- and the Russian. And I think it was 1940-- first of all. In 1939, I remember I did go look up-- again, I'm foolish [INAUDIBLE]. And I-- I really thinks. And they were one chasing each other. And I'm just looking at them, because then the Polish people, the Polish, whatever army they had. They didn't have an army. They have-- they have an army running bicycles, cavalry, cavalry running. They didn't have no, not mechanized. They maybe had five planes, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day, September 1, 1939, when the war started against Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, yeah, I remember. I think it didn't took-- I think it was altogether, I think Warsaw hold out for 15 days. But otherwise, crumbled on the Germans. You know, they were all mechanized. They just blow them out of the map.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the radio saying about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they say the truth in-- in BBC. They tell everything. And later on, in the beginning, they didn't say too much. I don't know why. But later on, as the war got intensified, they used to tell about everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were on the Russian side. so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you feel safe from the war? Did you feel that this was happening to-- happening to--

speaker: interviewer
text: --other people and you were safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, when the Russian come in, they-- they didn't discriminate against Jews, though. Every day, they opened all the schools. And anybody was willing to go to school, you could go to school. And you could do just about as far as a lot of things, libraries and a lot of cultural things.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I couldn't get a job because it was marked in my passport, I am a son of a businessman. Can you believe that? I-- and I was a young guy at that time. And they wouldn't give me a job for that reason. Did they take away your father's business, the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father give them the business. He had that brother what he had in Russia. And he told them, he said, you know? You want to have-- you want to have peace of mind, give him the business. What are you going to do it? They're going to get it anyway from you. So he gave him the business. And they made him in charge, you know. But he lasted-- he there, work for a while. And I don't know what happened. Yeah, then this-- that it was his business. They-- they told him he's stealing money from that. But he didn't. But they-- they took inventory. And they find out there's extra money or something. So they come to the conclusion, he was stealing money. So then they fired him from his business. But didn't-- they didn't pay nothing for that, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. And we are as far, and we still, you know, when they talk about war, we had a special room and they stacked up it a lot of meat and all kinds of things. And so we had enough to get by for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians came, you couldn't get a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what-- what was it your dream to do? If the war hadn't come, what did you want to do when you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably would have stayed in the same business, you know? I mean, then, you know, years of business, and there's only two-- two boys, like my father. And he was getting older. And he says, like he took over from his father, you will have to take over and run it from us, for you and your brother, whatever. But I think my brother, earlier, he was older, and he could get a job in Rowne. I wonder was he doing over there. I think he was greasing cars or whatever, trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a car in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no car.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say from 1939 to '41, your life went on pretty much the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: The life was-- was same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941 when the Germans come over, that's-- then you could hear already like 1941, this is, you know, at town so-and-so, they killed 100 people, here 50 people. But the people still wanted-- didn't want to believe it, though till it happened to them. Then everyone-- the rabbi said that everybody has to go and pray and everything would be OK. It didn't help, though. So more he tell the people to pray and not to go, you know, and the most people they survived whatever it was is younger people. And older people couldn't make it. Life was too harsh. Maybe there was some women, but very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1941, did your family talk about leaving and going somewhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Like I told you, they didn't nobody want to believe that such a thing exist. They thought maybe, ah, they-- it's over, going over-blowing. It's not going to happen to them. Otherwise, they would probably-- they had money. They probably would think maybe to flee the Poland and go to some other country or whatever. I don't know. But there never was a talk like that I could hear of this. Most-- most people in Poland left. If they were poor, they couldn't make a living. So they come over here in United States, or wherever, or Canada. And so that's what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day the Germans came into your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, let's see. In 1941, and the date, I don't remember exact. But anyway, they come in. They were very polite. But then the Ukrainian, they told them they were free and to do anything they want to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did they do? They-- they have another pogrom, and they killed people. And they got into houses and took their things. And after that, they-- they established a ghetto. I mean, but that was a small ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come in your house too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But by that time, was nothing left. Wasn't-- and they liked to take like furs. And-- and if you have gold or something, they would like to have gold and furs. Some of them neighbors, they knew you so good. And you would think, this is the people they're going to spare you. But they come in, and they were rough, you know? And some people, they killed too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anybody killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever seen anybody killed before this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But they come in, and if some people resisted them, they tell them they own everything. And they says, they-- every-- every Jew that a gentile talked to is-- is rich. And there was a lot of Jewish people. There was some-- what do you call it-- tailors and just regular working people. They didn't have no money. They had-- that's-- they struggled to-- to make a living. Well, I think most of them, they leave them-- they left them alone. They only did go to business people. If you had a business or something, they wanted. But later on, they just in '42, they liquidated, little by little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto, the ghetto was a very small ghetto. And everybody got along really pretty good there in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could stay in your same house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, yeah, yeah, in the same area. They just made an area in the same town, and most of it was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in your house, or did you have to move in another place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in my house, but there was restrictions. They want to have-- they want to-- first, they want to have-- they line up a bunch of people. And they says, well, and they have orders to have so much, so much money to raise by tomorrow. Otherwise, they're going to kill so many people. And so they had to come up. And once they come up, twice. And by the end, the people didn't have the money. So just-- they took every third or fourth person to line them up, and they just killed them. And it means a warning. The next time, they're going to kill all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They killed them right there where they were lined up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they line them up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the main square. And they lined them up, not-- not children, only grown-up people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only men that time. And later on, I think, in '42-- '42, yeah, was '42, then they started to liquidate it in the ghetto. And they did it in a fashion [INAUDIBLE]. And they said the harvest, they're going to give so much pounds of wheat, of flour. They're going to help the-- to harvest the wheat because otherwise, it's going to rot in the fields. And so my mother, I think, she was the first one to go. I didn't go and neither did my father. But she did go. And the first day, the first day, they give them wheat. Only when it's flour they given them, each ones. And the next-- they says, they need more people. They can use all the people they can get. And then when they get all them people, they just make them dig ditches, and they just the-- the Germans were-- the Germans were in charge, but the Ukrainian were using the guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- every-- every-- every-- what do you call it? Every ghetto had a-- a committee. But what smaller the town they were, they were not as-- as vicious. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: So in your town, they weren't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --very strong?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but they-- but they put a lot of-- they tell them where they want to every time from then. Most of what is they want is they want money. That's what they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an open ghetto or a closed ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a open, more or less one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who was the man who was in charge of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this one, probably, I blocked it out of my mind. I can't tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the Germans came in. It was July, '41. It was still summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the harvest was must have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: --October.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. In [INAUDIBLE]. I remember, it was [INAUDIBLE], in [INAUDIBLE] in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must-- are you sure '42, not '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was liquidated in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was liquidated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but that's when the last of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: In August.

speaker: interviewee
text: In August, the [INAUDIBLE]. That was the last one. My father lived till the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it's written about your town that there was a big massacre--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of people in October of '41. That would have been after [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around [INAUDIBLE], after [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. I even have a-- I made like a little bit like a-- and I marked it down. And-- how you say-- like a little matzevah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have it at home. And it's marked 1942, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your mother went to help with the harvest.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my mother went to help to collect the harvest. And they paid him really a good, because there was by that time, there wasn't enough food. And so they said the next morning, they can use more people. And then all the-- when they had enough people, they make him dig the ditches. And they machine gun them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this time, I went to that-- what do you call it? To the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The governess?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the governess. I didn't know that that would happen, but I did go for food. And then-- then everybody knew already that they massacred so many Jews. And she said to me, you know what? Maybe you shouldn't go back. We going to help you to get to the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my father, you know, years ago, we had a sukkah. And I-- and I used to open up the sukkah. And my father watch me the way I used to open the sukkah. So he hide behind the sukkah. And that's why he survived. Now, you know, after they-- after when they killed all the people, they go through town and look whom they can beside, clean everybody out as much as they can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about your brother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was-- my sister was hiding there in this-- under-- under the sukkah. I was not in town after that. And that's-- but people were there still alive. Some of them died in Israel. And they said to me, they seen they-- they took your sister out from there. But they didn't know what happened. I suppose it happened what with everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's alive here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where was he at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, he was too. He run away with the other group in the-- in the woods. I didn't know about him till the war was over. I didn't have no contact with him. I know when the war was over, what was it, 1945. I mean, I didn't met him till 1946. 1946? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this day when you went by your governess--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the last time you saw--

speaker: interviewee
text: The last time.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the last time I saw my father. I didn't go back there no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the last time you saw anybody until you were reunited with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brother. And my brother, he was in other part of the woods. And I didn't know. And I didn't even know if he was alive or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you by the governess?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long because she told me that neighbors were telling that she's hiding some Jewish people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she hiding out the Jews beside you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but, you know, they were telling. And she said, and they catch me, she said, she don't mind. But they're going to kill us all. So why don't I take you. And she said, there is some partisan there in their woods. Maybe you can connect with them. [CRYING] [SOBS] [SOBS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] that happened. OK, let's go on, Phyllis. Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took you to the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she took me to the forest. And she told me where the other ones are hiding there. And, you know, got by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about how that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more details how that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she-- she was-- she-- because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the forest? How long was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Miedzyrzecz is not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from her house?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, Miedzyrzecz, Rowne. No, no, not Rowne. It's not Rowne. What is it? Hm. What did I written down there? I forgot. I know I says I was in the woods there. I seen there, not in this part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she took you there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you hook up with people?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, she told me where they are approximately. In most of it, in the woods, there was nobody else there. Nobody-- most of them had afraid to get in there. If you are not, let's say, if you are Ukrainian or a Nazi or that, they tried a couple times to penetrate. But we were very deep in the woods, and they were afraid to get in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there? How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some soldiers, were some Russian soldiers who had run away. And there was some [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jews. There were-- and that's about it. There were some Russian and Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you just like that? Did you have to prove yourself to them in any way

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And there is only two-- they know I was desperate because is only Jewish and-- is only Jewish and gentile. And they know. It's not like over here. You can hide your identity. They know if you're Jewish or not. And they knew what was going on and why you're there, because you're running away. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of the other partisans you were with in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: And for most of it from the same town when I am, Miedzyrzecz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do You remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they're in-- they're in Israel, some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us the names that you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spiegelman and then what are the other guy? I think it was four or five, four or five people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and so most of it were like neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spiegelman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spiegelman. And one was Chaim, Chaim Sternsczs. But he's not a relative. He just another-- he lived in the same town. But he was no relative of mine or whatever. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is probably the hardest part to talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know. It was very hard. And then by the end of the war, I think, I got-- I got hit with the shrapnel. And-- and I couldn't-- I was no use to them no more, for I mean, basically, you know? You didn't-- I couldn't do nothing. I had to be incapacitated in the war. How did you get hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were shooting of us because we're running away. And they hit me right in the-- hit me right here and knocked out a couple ribs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who shot you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must be the Germans because we run away. We went after one mission and it was unsuccessful. And they chase you all. They chase them. But I'm the only guy got hit. All the other one run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the mission?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mission was we want to get-- get rid of a couple of Germans and take their guns. And it didn't work, and it backfired.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you going to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a vehicle. You see? I-- and see, I can't remember even that. And this is-- this is next to Miedzyrzecz and them. It's a forest, you know? In Poland, there was a lot of big forest with wood. And I couldn't even remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this time that you wanted to get the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We thought we were going where we could have more guns. We could operate a better, a more efficient, you know? And there was not, maybe 10 guns and 100 and some people. It was not enough guns. But, I mean, a lot of-- and then-- and then the war, I notice it. And people got malnutrition and they got sick. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: But going back to this mission--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that backfired--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tell us a little bit about it. What was the plan?

speaker: interviewee
text: A plan is to get more guns so we can operate more efficient and-- and go out and get food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you going to get the guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: From them, from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were by the railroad station. And it's-- they are-- and they-- what do you call it? There are certain section of the railroad station. They were-- what do you call-- they were guarding. And we though this was like 10 of them or 12. And we thought that we can get only three of them, we would be OK. But somehow, it backfired.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how. That backfired. They started shooting at us. And everybody, we were on-- and you know, you couldn't-- if you don't have enough guns, and you know you're going to, so some of them took off. I was the only guy who got hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were ready for you. Do you think somebody tipped them off you were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. You don't have no-- nobody has a radio or anything. How you're going to tell them? I mean, here you have radios. There was no radios or any communication. You couldn't talk on the outside world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the beginning when you got to the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a morning. You were home. And then you went to this nanny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had no more home.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: No more home after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all on your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. But, I mean, the same token--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you know that your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew what my mother was killed. And we got [INAUDIBLE] communication with it, with the town. And they told me my father was alive. From everybody, my father was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then do you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got killed too. And did it. It was two shooters. The one was-- the one was-- like I told you, one was [INAUDIBLE] and one was [INAUDIBLE]. He was the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you got to the forest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you live there? Before you lived in a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you have no more house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to go out by the farmers and beg for food. Some of them, they used to have like potatoes. And they didn't maybe want to give it to you. But they put on the potatoes outside. So if something happened, they're going to tell, you know, the German catch them? They're going to tell, he had the potatoes laid out for the-- for the animals to feed them. And, well, in the beginning, it was very tough till we got organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a bunker. And they built a big bunker and dug up a lot of dirt. And you were-- you know? It wasn't bad under there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the bunker was underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did it have any kind of furniture in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you kidding? It just a-- a double or triple bed, whatever you could arrange it. Wouldn't be anything. But I don't know what they put on the top because it didn't leak, though. The water didn't come through. I don't know what [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were in the bunker with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- they had three of them, not all in this, in the same area. But one didn't have a connection with the other. Let's see, I think about 25, 30 people in each, in each bunker. Everybody-- but there was nothing, was no-- I think they had one table, yeah, one table. And there's no bedding, if that's what you mean. You sleep with your clothes. You have to sleep. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Uh-oh.
